text: Today, we will present an interesting case of a large retrorectal schwannoma resected via a minimally invasive approach. The patient is a forty-nine-year-old female evaluated for a large pelvic mass found incidentally during attempted IUD exchange. She was largely asymptomatic, but a thorough review of systems identified a year-long history of urinary incontinence as well as abdominal distension and bloating. She denied any changes in bowel habits. Her history was significant for prior abdominoplasty. A preoperative CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a large heterogeneous retrorectal mass. A pelvic MRI further demonstrated a bilobed solid mass with cystic changes or necrosis. There was no evidence of invasion into surrounding structures or pathologic lymph nodes. There was impressive anterior displacement of the bladder, uterus, and rectum, which is circled in red in this image. A CT-guided parasacral biopsy demonstrated a low-grade spindle cell neoplasm consistent with schwannoma. Given its location and size, we opted to approach this resection transabdominally using the XI robot.

The patient is positioned for a pelvic procedure with the robot set on the left side. Ports are placed transversely above the umbilicus. A large pelvic mass is visible upon abdominal entry and insufflation. The uterus is retracted anteriorly for better visualization. The retroperitoneum is opened to the right of the mass, and Firefly technology is used to identify the ureter. Dissection proceeds laterally to expose the right side of the mass, with the sigmoid colon displaced anterolaterally. The left ureter is visualized along the pelvic sidewall. The mass is freed circumferentially at its superior aspect, allowing for increased mobility during distal dissection.

The surgeon performs lateral and interior dissection down to the pelvic floor and distal extent of the large mass. A digital rectal exam confirms the location. Posterior dissection is challenging due to the mass's size and adhesions. After reaching the dissection limit robotically, a fan and steel incision is made to complete mobilization and remove the tumor intact.

Gross examination of the resected tumor revealed a bilode mass with both solid and cystic components. The patient recovered well after surgery and was discharged on postoperative day one. Her final pathology demonstrated a completely excised, fifteen by ten by ten centimeter schwannoma.

Retrorectal or presacral tumors are an uncommon clinical entity. They are typically found incidentally during routine physical exam, with the most common presenting symptom being a vague and chronic lower back or perineal pain. They are more common in women and are typically cystic in nature, with the exception of chordomas, which are the most common solid tumor and have a slightly higher incidence in males. Most retrorectal tumors are congenital and about half are benign, although some benign lesions do carry malignant potential. In the large majority of cases, surgery, either via a transabdominal or perineal approach, is the mainstay of treatment. The retrorectal space is a location where several different types of embryologically distinct cell lines converge, which leads to a wide variety of lesions in this area. A treatment algorithm for the workup and management of these complex, yet rare tumors can be found in the ASCRS manual of colon and rectal surgery.
